An integration plan should focus on the three points of integration, cultural, operational, and financial. In order to get the most leverage out of your newly acquired business, you need to foster an environment that creates an engaged team. Executing a growth strategy requires a team that is aligned culturally, operationally, and financially. Otherwise, your new business will be capped on its potential. According to the Gallup poll, out of 100 million workers, two-thirds of all employees surveyed aren't actively engaged in their work. Think about it, 100 million people, two-thirds are not engaged in their work. That's potentially two-thirds of your workforce asleep at the will of your business working for a seller that didn't care. But you're a buyer who does. So now what you need to do is you need to take control of where you want your business to go. And this is very important because your team has been trained probably not to focus on those elements of the business. Now how do you think that this current level of potential engagement in your business affects customers, affects new employees potentially, affects the bank account, right? Disengagement is a distraction that can cause a disaster for a business. The faster you can get engagement, positive engagement, okay, so you need to connect with your people. The faster you can do that and weed out the ones that are disengaged, the faster you can get your business built creating significant value. Now what do you think you should do? You can't just go tell them to be more engaged. That's not leadership and it's never going to work, especially with new people that you don't know. That's abdication of responsibility. When you just go to people and say you figure it out, that's abdication. I'm going to give you a little bit of a leadership lesson here since you're a new business owner. The sooner you can take responsibility for the success of your business and the success of your team, the faster you can create value in your business. And how do you take control of your business? You need to help each and every one of your team members understand why their role is so important and how their performance directly impacts the health of the organization but also the growth of the organization. Not just for them but for you, the other employees and everyone that your businesses touch. Inspire and motivate people by aligning your expectation for their results to the mission, vision and core values of the new business that you just acquired. People don't want jobs anymore. They want careers. They want opportunities to learn, grow, achieve, bigger things, do more things, grow as a human being. And the predominant way they do that is through their work because it monetizes their growth. But this doesn't have to fall just on you. Of course, you need to set very clear expectations for performance and you need to incentivize them accordingly when they reach those targets. Show them that when the business wins, they win. Teach them when they win, the business needs to win too. If you just organize that with your team, my guess is they probably never had that kind of interaction before. So if you just do that, the ones that understand how to create value will help you build your new business. Now, to do this, you need to first integrate with the business you've just acquired and fast. In the next module, you'll hear from one of my acquisition mentees, Aaron Christensen. He described some of the hard-learn lessons acquiring a string of automotive repair shops in Las Vegas because he didn't have the best integration plan. He was able to overcome most of the challenges but with a better integration strategy, he would have been able to overcome more of them. In the next few lessons, I'll cover each area you'll need to integrate.
